keyPoints: EMT involves loss of epithelial characteristics and acquisition of mesenchymal traits.
Occurs during development, wound healing, tissue regeneration, and fibrosis.
Malignant cells undergoing EMT retain unregulated proliferation capabilities.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · METASTASIS (part 1)

items.title: Targeting Metastasis in Cancer Therapy
items.content: Few drugs directly target metastasis due to the complexity of identifying critical steps. However, HER2 is a known enhancer of metastatic potential in breast cancer, and trastuzumab targets HER2 to improve survival.
keyPoints: HER2 enhances metastatic potential in breast cancer.
Trastuzumab (monoclonal antibody) improves survival for HER2-positive patients.
Potential drug targets include HIF-1/-2, cMET, VEGFR, SRC, FAK, matrix metalloproteinases 1 and 2, COX-2.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · METASTASIS (part 1)

items.title: Mechanism and Management of Bone Metastases
items.content: Bone metastases involve malignant cells disrupting the balance between osteoclasts and osteoblasts, primarily through increased RANK signaling. This leads to bone resorption and structural damage. Therapeutic interventions like denosumab (an antibody against RANKL) and bisphosphonates inhibit osteoclast activity, preventing further bone destruction.
keyPoints: Bone metastases cause pain, fractures, hypercalcemia, and morbidity.
RANK/RANKL/OPG pathway is crucial in bone resorption by osteoclasts.
Tumors increase RANK signaling via substances like PTH, IL1, or Mip1.
Denosumab inhibits RANKL to prevent bone destruction.
Bisphosphonates are used to inhibit osteoclast function in cancer patients with bone metastases.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · METASTASIS (part 2) | CANCER STEM CELLS

items.title: Role of Cancer Stem Cells in Tumor Growth and Resistance
items.content: A small subset of cells within tumors, known as cancer stem cells, possess self-renewal capabilities similar to normal stem cells. These cells are less responsive to chemotherapy and radiation due to slow cell cycle progression and high expression of antiapoptotic proteins and drug efflux pumps.
keyPoints: Cancer stem cells have unlimited proliferative capacity but progress slowly through the cell cycle.
They express high levels of antiapoptotic Bcl-2 family members and MDR family drug efflux pumps.
<1% of AML and CML cells are cancer stem cells with properties like Thy1-CD34+CD38-.
Cancer growth is driven by expansion of the stem cell pool and failure of apoptosis pathways.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · METASTASIS (part 2) | CANCER STEM CELLS

items.title: Implications for Cancer Treatment Targeting Stem Cells
items.content: Current cancer therapies often fail to eradicate tumors because they do not effectively target cancer stem cells. Research is focused on identifying and isolating these cells to understand their unique signaling pathways, which could serve as potential therapeutic targets.
keyPoints: Failure of current therapies may be due to ineffectiveness against cancer stem cells.
Cancer stem cells can arise from other epithelial cells through processes like epithelial mesenchymal transition.
Targeting aberrant signaling pathways in cancer stem cells could improve treatment efficacy.
Treating all cancer cells, not just those with stem cell-like properties, is crucial for eliminating the self-renewing population.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · METASTASIS (part 2) | CANCER STEM CELLS

items.title: Why Cancer Cells Develop Resistance to Therapy
items.content: Cancer cells exhibit significant plasticity, allowing them to adapt and develop resistance to therapies such as chemotherapy and radiation. This adaptation is facilitated by the heterogeneity within tumor cell populations and their interactions with the microenvironment.
keyPoints: Cancer stem cells can alter multiple aspects of cell biology in response to external factors like chemotherapy and inflammation.
Tumor heterogeneity contributes to plasticity, aiding resistance to both internal and external stresses.

Mechanisms of resistance include inhibiting drug delivery, blocking uptake/retention, increasing metabolism, altering target protein levels/sensitivity, acquiring mutations, modifying signaling pathways, adjusting replication processes, inhibiting apoptosis, and evading the immune system.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · PLASTICITY AND RESISTANCE

items.title: How to Address Resistance in Metastatic Cancers
items.content: Most metastatic cancers develop resistance to therapy over time due to their plasticity. Understanding and overcoming these resistance mechanisms is crucial for effective treatment.
keyPoints: Metastatic cancers, except those curable with chemotherapy like germ cell tumors, typically become resistant to ongoing therapies.
Research focuses on overcoming resistance by targeting the diverse mechanisms cancer cells use to adapt.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · PLASTICITY AND RESISTANCE

items.title: Warburg Effect in Cancer Metabolism
items.content: Cancer cells exhibit altered metabolism, notably the Warburg effect, where they preferentially utilize aerobic glycolysis over oxidative phosphorylation. This leads to increased glucose uptake and lactic acid production.
keyPoints: Increased glucose uptake by cancer cells is utilized in FDG-PET scanning for tumor detection.
Proteins like cMYC, HIF1, RAS, p53, pRB, and AKT modulate glycolytic processes.
Inhibitors targeting HIF1 and KRAS12C pathways are approved for specific cancers.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · CANCER METABOLISM

items.title: Role of Glutamine in Cancer Metabolism
items.content: Cancer cells also rely on glutamine as an alternative metabolic pathway, similar to glucose, providing both structural molecules and energy.
keyPoints: Glutamine is inefficiently utilized by cancer cells.
Nutrient uptake from surrounding tissues adds complexity to targeting cancer metabolism.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · CANCER METABOLISM

items.title: Mutations in Metabolic Genes and Therapeutic Implications
items.content: Mutations in metabolic genes like IDH1 and IDH2 lead to the production of oncometabolites such as 2-hydroxyglutarate, affecting DNA methylation and cellular processes.
keyPoints: IDH1/2 mutations are common in gliomas, AMLs, and intrahepatic cholangiocarcinomas.
Inhibitors of mutant IDH1/2 are approved for treating specific cancers with these mutations.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · CANCER METABOLISM

items.subtopic: TUMOR MICROENVIRONMENT, ANGIOGENESIS, AND IMMUNE EVASION | OBESITY AND CANCER
items.title: Mechanisms of Tumor Microenvironment in Immune Evasion and Drug Resistance
items.content: The tumor microenvironment is a dynamic and complex network involving various cell types and factors that contribute to immune evasion and drug resistance. This environment facilitates altered metabolism, growth, metastasis, and phenotypic plasticity of tumors.
keyPoints: Tumor microenvironment includes lymphocytes, macrophages, myeloid cells, vascular cells, nerve cells, fibroblasts, fat cells, ECM, stroma, secreted factors, reactive oxygen/nitrogen species, mechanical factors, blood vessels, and lymphatics.
Cross-talk between these components enhances tumor maintenance, growth, metastasis, immune escape, and resistance to anticancer therapies.
Mechanisms of immune evasion include suppression of effector T cells, increase in regulatory T cells, induction of an inflammatory environment, and altered vasculature inhibiting T-cell access.

Drug resistance mechanisms involve alterations in metabolic pathways creating hypoxic/acidic environments, vascular/mechanical factors limiting drug access, secreted factors inhibiting apoptosis or stimulating survival pathways, and generation of ROS enhancing drug resistance.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · TUMOR MICROENVIRONMENT, ANGIOGENESIS, AND IMMUNE EVASION | OBESITY AND CANCER

items.subtopic: TUMOR MICROENVIRONMENT, ANGIOGENESIS, AND IMMUNE EVASION | OBESITY AND CANCER
items.title: Impact of Obesity on Cancer Risk and Outcomes
items.content: Obesity is linked to an increased risk of various cancers due to hormonal changes, altered metabolism, and inflammatory mediators. It affects cancer development and survival outcomes, with complex associations observed in different cancer types.
keyPoints: Obesity increases the risk for postmenopausal breast, colorectal, ovarian, endometrial, esophageal, gallbladder, thyroid, and kidney cancers.
Mechanisms include high levels of insulin, glucagon, leptin, insulin resistance increasing IGF-1 levels, altered adipose metabolism producing compounds important for energy and membrane function.
Obesity leads to an inflammatory environment with increased IL-6 and TNF-alpha levels.
In breast cancer, obesity is associated with decreased survival due to hormonal impacts; paradoxically, it may improve survival in advanced-stage colorectal cancer patients.
Immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy appears more effective in obese patients.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · TUMOR MICROENVIRONMENT, ANGIOGENESIS, AND IMMUNE EVASION | OBESITY AND CANCER

items.title: The Role of Hypoxia-Inducible Factors in Angiogenesis
items.content: Hypoxia-inducible factors (HIFs), particularly HIF1 and HIF2, play crucial roles in tumor angiogenesis. HIF1 primarily stimulates metabolic processes like glycogenesis, while HIF2 is more involved in promoting angiogenesis by inducing the transcription of genes such as VEGF.
keyPoints: Hypoxia-inducible factors (HIFs), especially 1 and 2, are central to the angiogenic response.
HIF1 stimulates metabolism; HIF2 is more involved in angiogenesis.
Mutations in von Hippel-Lindau tumor suppressor gene can enhance HIF function, affecting VEGF expression.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · MECHANISMS OF TUMOR VESSEL FORMATION (part 1)

items.title: VEGF and its Receptors in Angiogenesis
items.content: Vascular endothelial growth factors (VEGFs) are critical for both normal and pathological angiogenesis. VEGF-A, with its isoforms, binds to receptors VEGFR1 and VEGFR2 on endothelial cells, influencing their proliferation, migration, and survival.
keyPoints: VEGFs are crucial for embryonic vasculogenesis and pathologic conditions like tumor angiogenesis.
VEGF-A has multiple isoforms that regulate blood vessel formation by binding to VEGFR1 and VEGFR2.
VEGFR2 directly regulates endothelial cell processes, while VEGFR1 has nuanced roles.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · MECHANISMS OF TUMOR VESSEL FORMATION (part 1)

items.title: Role of Ang1 and Ang2 in Tumor Angiogenesis
items.content: Ang1 promotes vessel stability by binding to the EC RTK Tie-2, enhancing interactions with ECM and perivascular cells. In contrast, Ang2 destabilizes vessels by inhibiting Ang1's action, making them more responsive to VEGF.
keyPoints: Ang1 binds to Tie-2, promoting vessel stability and preventing VEGF-induced permeability.
Ang2 acts as a competitive inhibitor of Ang1, facilitating vascular remodeling in response to angiogenic signals.
Tumor vessels become leaky and hemorrhagic due to the absence of stabilization by the Ang1/Tie2 interaction.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · MECHANISMS OF TUMOR VESSEL FORMATION (part 2)

items.title: Integrin Expression in Angiogenic Endothelium
items.content: Angiogenic endothelium overexpresses integrins such as αvβ3, which are crucial for EC adhesion, migration, and survival, facilitating angiogenesis.
keyPoints: Integrins like αvβ3 mediate EC spreading and migration essential for VEGF- and bFGF-induced angiogenesis.
αvβ3 integrin associates with VEGFR2 to promote EC proliferation and survival through various signaling pathways.
Complexes of αvβ3 with MMPs enhance EC migration by cleaving ECM proteins.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · MECHANISMS OF TUMOR VESSEL FORMATION (part 2)

items.title: Abnormalities in Tumor Blood Vessels
items.content: Tumor blood vessels exhibit chaotic architecture and flow due to an imbalance of angiogenic regulators, contributing to poor leukocyte-endothelial interactions.
keyPoints: Tumor vessels are tortuous and dilated because of imbalanced angiogenic factors like VEGFs and angiopoietins.
Chaotic blood flow in tumor vessels leads to poor leukocyte-endothelial interactions, aiding immune evasion by tumor cells.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · MECHANISMS OF TUMOR VESSEL FORMATION (part 2)

items.title: Tumor Blood Flow Variability and Implications for Treatment
items.content: Tumor blood flow is highly variable, with regions experiencing hypoxemia and acidosis. This environment selects for cancer cell variants resistant to apoptosis due to the loss of p53 expression. The structural abnormalities in tumor vessels, such as uneven diameter, excessive branching, shunting, and high permeability, contribute to increased interstitial pressure within tumors. These factors interfere with effective delivery of therapeutics.
keyPoints: Tumor blood flow is variable, leading to hypoxemia and acidosis.
Cancer cells may lose p53 expression, becoming resistant to apoptosis.
Increased interstitial pressure interferes with therapeutic delivery.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · MECHANISMS OF TUMOR VESSEL FORMATION (part 3)

items.title: Tumor Angiogenesis and Antiangiogenic Therapy
items.content: During tumor angiogenesis, endothelial cells (ECs) are highly proliferative and express plasma membrane proteins characteristic of activated endothelium. These include growth factor receptors and adhesion molecules like integrins. The structural differences in tumor vasculature compared to normal vessels provide differential sensitivities that can be targeted by antiangiogenic agents.
keyPoints: ECs in tumors are highly proliferative with unique protein expressions.
Tumor vessels have a mosaic of ECs and tumor cells, forming vascular mimicry.
Antiangiogenic therapy targets these abnormal vessel characteristics.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · MECHANISMS OF TUMOR VESSEL FORMATION (part 3)

items.title: Use of Bevacizumab in Various Cancers
items.content: Bevacizumab, an anti-VEGF antibody, enhances the efficacy of several chemotherapeutic regimens across different cancers such as colon, lung, ovarian, and cervical. It also shows activity with immunotherapy for renal cell carcinomas (RCCs) and is used alone in treating glioblastomas.
keyPoints: Bevacizumab binds circulating VEGF.
Used to treat colon, lung, ovarian, cervical cancers, RCCs with immunotherapy, and glioblastomas.
Common side effect: hypertension; can be managed with antihypertensives.
Rare but serious risks include arterial thromboembolic events, hemorrhage, bowel perforation, and inhibition of wound healing.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · ANTIANGIOGENIC THERAPY

items.title: Combination Therapy with Anti-VEGF Agents and Immunomodulators
items.content: Anti-VEGF agents like bevacizumab have shown enhanced activity when used in combination with immunomodulators, such as immune checkpoint inhibitors. Approved combinations include durvalumab plus bevacizumab for hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) and lenvatinib plus pembrolizumab for endometrial cancer and RCC.
keyPoints: Combination therapy enhances anti-VEGF efficacy.
Approved combinations: durvalumab + bevacizumab for HCC; lenvatinib + pembrolizumab for endometrial cancer and RCC.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · ANTIANGIOGENIC THERAPY

items.subtopic: EVASION OF THE IMMUNE SYSTEM BY CANCERS (part 1)
items.title: Mechanisms of Immune Evasion by Cancers
items.content: Cancer cells employ various strategies to evade immune detection and destruction. These include downregulation of cell surface proteins involved in immune recognition, expression of immunosuppressive molecules, secretion of immunosuppressive factors like TGF-β, recruitment of regulatory T cells, induction of T-cell tolerance, and downregulation of death receptors.
keyPoints: Cancer cells evade the immune system through mechanisms such as downregulation of MHC proteins and tumor-specific antigens.
Expression of immunosuppressive molecules like PD-L1 inhibits immune function.
Secretion of TGF-β and recruitment of regulatory T cells contribute to an immunosuppressive environment.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · EVASION OF THE IMMUNE SYSTEM BY CANCERS (part 1)

items.subtopic: EVASION OF THE IMMUNE SYSTEM BY CANCERS (part 1)
items.title: Factors Influencing Response to Immunotherapy
items.content: The effectiveness of immunotherapeutic approaches varies among different malignancies. Factors influencing this response include tumor mutational burden, presence of neoantigens, expression of immune checkpoint proteins like PD-L1, density of tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes, and host genetic factors.
keyPoints: Melanomas, RCC, Merkel cell carcinomas, and certain lymphomas respond well to immunotherapy.
Tumor mutational burden and neoantigen presence are important for immunotherapy response.
PD-L1 expression is a predictive marker for treatment decisions in specific cancers like non-small-cell lung cancer.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · EVASION OF THE IMMUNE SYSTEM BY CANCERS (part 1)

items.subtopic: EVASION OF THE IMMUNE SYSTEM BY CANCERS (part 1)
items.title: Need for Better Biomarkers in Immunotherapy
items.content: Current biomarkers, such as PD-L1 expression, are insufficient to predict immunotherapy responsiveness across most tumors. Research is focused on identifying new biomarkers and strategies to convert non-responsive cancers into responsive ones.
keyPoints: PD-L1 expression alone cannot predict responsiveness for most tumors.
There is a need for better biomarkers to define potential responsiveness of specific cancers to immunotherapy.
Research aims to develop approaches that make non-responsive cancers amenable to immunotherapy.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · EVASION OF THE IMMUNE SYSTEM BY CANCERS (part 1)

items.subtopic: EVASION OF THE IMMUNE SYSTEM BY CANCERS (part 2)
items.title: CAR-T Cell Therapy: Efficacy and Challenges
items.content: Chimeric antigen receptor T-cell (CAR-T) therapy involves reengineering a patient's T cells to target specific antigens on tumor cells, such as CD19 for certain lymphomas. This approach has shown significant antitumor activity, particularly in treating acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL) and diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL), achieving durable remissions even in patients resistant to standard therapies.
keyPoints: CAR-T cells target antigens like CD19 on ALL and DLBCL.
Approved for use in ALL, DLBCL, follicular lymphoma, and mantle cell lymphoma.
Significant antitumor activity with durable remissions reported.
Toxicities include cytokine release syndrome, organ toxicity, neurotoxicity, and potential T-cell malignancies.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · EVASION OF THE IMMUNE SYSTEM BY CANCERS (part 2)

items.subtopic: EVASION OF THE IMMUNE SYSTEM BY CANCERS (part 2)
items.title: Immune Checkpoint Inhibitors: Mechanisms and Clinical Use
items.content: Immune checkpoint inhibitors work by blocking proteins that inhibit T-cell activation, such as CTLA-4, PD-1, PD-L1, and LAG3. These therapies release the 'brakes' on the immune system, allowing for enhanced antitumor activity against cancers that exploit these checkpoints to evade immune detection.
keyPoints: Target proteins involved in T-cell inhibition: CTLA-4, PD-1, PD-L1, and LAG3.
Approved monoclonal antibodies enhance T-cell activation by blocking checkpoint interactions.
Neoantigens on cancer cells facilitate T-cell activation against tumors.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · EVASION OF THE IMMUNE SYSTEM BY CANCERS (part 2)

items.subtopic: EVASION OF THE IMMUNE SYSTEM BY CANCERS (part 2)
items.title: Tumor-Infiltrating Lymphocyte Therapy: Current Status and Limitations
items.content: Tumor-infiltrating lymphocyte therapy involves using autologous T cells derived from a patient's tumor, expanded with IL-2, to treat cancers like melanoma. While approved for melanoma, expansion challenges may limit its broader application.
keyPoints: Approved for melanoma treatment in combination with IL-2.
Potential future approval for other cancers such as RCC.
Technical issues include inadequate cell expansion.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · EVASION OF THE IMMUNE SYSTEM BY CANCERS (part 2)

items.subtopic: EVASION OF THE IMMUNE SYSTEM BY CANCERS (part 3)
items.title: Role of Immune Checkpoint Inhibitors in Cancer Treatment
items.content: Immune checkpoint inhibitors target co-inhibitory molecules like CTLA-4, PD-1, and PD-L1 to enhance immune response against cancer cells. These inhibitors are approved for various cancers including melanoma, RCC, lung cancer, and more.
keyPoints: Monoclonal antibody against CTLA-4 is approved for melanoma and other malignancies.
Antibodies targeting PD-1 or PD-L1 are approved for multiple cancers such as melanoma, RCC, NSCLC, HCC, etc.
Combination of anti-CTLA-4 and anti-PD-1 antibodies approved for melanoma, RCC, HCC, NSCLC, mesothelioma, and MSI-high metastatic colorectal cancers.
High PD-L1 expression and increased neoantigens (MSI-high, TMB-high) are determinants of better response.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · EVASION OF THE IMMUNE SYSTEM BY CANCERS (part 3)

items.subtopic: EVASION OF THE IMMUNE SYSTEM BY CANCERS (part 3)
items.title: Determinants of Response to Immune Checkpoint Inhibitors
items.content: The effectiveness of immune checkpoint inhibitors is influenced by factors such as PD-L1 expression and the presence of neoantigens, which are more prevalent in MSI-high and TMB-high cancers.
keyPoints: High PD-L1 expression correlates with better responses to checkpoint inhibitors.
Increased neoantigens in tumors (MSI-high, TMB-high) enhance response to treatment.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · EVASION OF THE IMMUNE SYSTEM BY CANCERS (part 3)

items.subtopic: Cancer Cell Biology | CANCER CELL BIOLOGY
items.question: Which type of cancer is derived from epithelial tissue?
items.explanation: Cancers derived from epithelial tissue are called carcinomas.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · Cancer Cell Biology | CANCER CELL BIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Cancer Cell Biology | CANCER CELL BIOLOGY
items.question: What is the initial origin of most cancers?
items.explanation: Most cancers arise as a consequence of genetic alterations beginning in a single cell, making them monoclonal.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · Cancer Cell Biology | CANCER CELL BIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Cancer Cell Biology | CANCER CELL BIOLOGY
items.question: What is the role of oncogenes?
options: They promote cell growth when altered.
items.explanation: Oncogenes are genes that can promote cell growth when altered.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · Cancer Cell Biology | CANCER CELL BIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Cancer Cell Biology | CANCER CELL BIOLOGY
items.question: Which of the following is NOT a characteristic of normal adult cells?
items.explanation: Normal adult cells do not have uncontrolled proliferation or invasion capabilities.

items.subtopic: DIFFERENCES BETWEEN PEDIATRIC AND ADULT CANCERS (part 1)
items.question: Which of the following statements best describes a key difference between pediatric and adult cancers?
options: Pediatric cancers generally have higher mutational burdens than adult cancers.
Childhood cancers tend to be more responsive to chemotherapy compared to adult cancers.
Environmental or lifestyle risk factors are more prevalent in childhood cancers.
The growth fraction of pediatric tumors is usually greater than 50% at diagnosis.
items.explanation: Childhood cancers generally tend to be much more responsive to chemotherapy than adult cancers with significantly higher rates of cure.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · DIFFERENCES BETWEEN PEDIATRIC AND ADULT CANCERS (part 1)

items.subtopic: DIFFERENCES BETWEEN PEDIATRIC AND ADULT CANCERS (part 1)
items.question: What is a common characteristic of the growth kinetics of human tumors?
options: Tumors grow exponentially without any decline in growth rate.
All daughter cells produced by tumor cell division are actively dividing.
The growth fraction of tumors declines over time due to various factors.
Human tumors become clinically detectable when they reach a size of about 10^11 cells.
items.explanation: The growth fraction of a tumor declines with time, largely due to factors in the microenvironment and accumulation of genetic damage over time.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · DIFFERENCES BETWEEN PEDIATRIC AND ADULT CANCERS (part 1)

items.subtopic: DIFFERENCES BETWEEN PEDIATRIC AND ADULT CANCERS (part 1)
items.question: Which factor contributes to the decreased proliferation of tumor cells over time?
options: Tumor cells increase their capacity for apoptosis.
The microenvironment provides an abundance of nutrients and energy.
Normal cells in the microenvironment can slow down cancer cell growth.
Genetic lesions in tumor cells promote a decrease in genetic damage.
items.explanation: Normal cells and other factors in the microenvironment can contribute to slowing down the growth of cancer cells over time.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · DIFFERENCES BETWEEN PEDIATRIC AND ADULT CANCERS (part 1)

items.subtopic: DIFFERENCES BETWEEN PEDIATRIC AND ADULT CANCERS (part 1)
items.question: What is a significant difference between how bone marrow and tumors respond to cell number reduction?
options: Bone marrow increases its growth rate indefinitely after cell number reduction.
Tumors stop growing when they reach their production goals.
Bone marrow stops growing once it has reached its production goals, unlike tumors.
Both bone marrow and tumors increase proliferation in response to reduced cell numbers.
items.explanation: The critical difference is that the marrow stops growing when it has reached its production goals, whereas tumors do not.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · DIFFERENCES BETWEEN PEDIATRIC AND ADULT CANCERS (part 1)

items.subtopic: DIFFERENCES BETWEEN PEDIATRIC AND ADULT CANCERS (part 2)
items.question: Which phase of the cell cycle is mentioned as potentially influencing the process of cell extrusion?
items.explanation: The passage mentions that the process of cell extrusion may depend on cell cycle arrest in the S phase.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · DIFFERENCES BETWEEN PEDIATRIC AND ADULT CANCERS (part 2)

items.subtopic: DIFFERENCES BETWEEN PEDIATRIC AND ADULT CANCERS (part 2)
items.question: What is a major role of cell extrusion in normal tissues?
items.explanation: Cell extrusion plays a major role in the elimination of both cells that are no longer needed by the organ and cells that are damaged and potentially dangerous.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · DIFFERENCES BETWEEN PEDIATRIC AND ADULT CANCERS (part 2)

items.subtopic: DIFFERENCES BETWEEN PEDIATRIC AND ADULT CANCERS (part 2)
items.question: Which process is NOT mentioned as contributing to defects in appropriate cell extrusion?
options: Apoptosis and other mechanisms of cell death
items.explanation: The passage mentions alterations in cell cycle control, apoptosis, and uncontrolled cell signaling as contributing to defects in cell extrusion, but not enhanced mechanical processes.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · DIFFERENCES BETWEEN PEDIATRIC AND ADULT CANCERS (part 2)

items.question: Which phase of the cell cycle involves assessing whether the genome has been appropriately duplicated and is ready to divide?
items.explanation: The $ G_{2}/M $ boundary assesses genome duplication readiness before division.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · CELL CYCLE CHECKPOINTS (part 1)

items.question: Which protein is primarily responsible for halting the cell cycle at the $ G_{1} $ checkpoint until the cell is ready?
items.explanation: The retinoblastoma protein (Rb) places a brake on the cell cycle at $ G_{1} $.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · CELL CYCLE CHECKPOINTS (part 1)

items.question: What is the role of p53 in response to DNA damage detected before mitosis?
options: Halts cell cycle progression and may initiate apoptosis
items.explanation: p53 halts cell cycle progression and can initiate apoptosis if damage is irreparable.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · CELL CYCLE CHECKPOINTS (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following proteins competes with p53 for binding to mdm2, allowing p53 accumulation?
items.explanation: p14ARF competes with p53 for mdm2 binding, facilitating p53 accumulation.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · CELL CYCLE CHECKPOINTS (part 1)

items.question: Which protein is overexpressed in malignant cells leading to the inactivation of p53?
items.explanation: Overexpression of the inhibitory mdm2 protein leads to the inactivation of p53.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · CELL CYCLE CHECKPOINTS (part 2)

items.question: Which virus oncogene acts to increase the rapid turnover of p53?
items.explanation: The human papillomavirus E6 oncogene increases the rapid turnover of p53.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · CELL CYCLE CHECKPOINTS (part 2)

items.question: What is a primary function of the spindle checkpoint during cell division?
options: Ensures proper attachment of chromosomes to the mitotic spindle
items.explanation: The spindle checkpoint ensures proper attachment of chromosomes to the mitotic spindle before cell cycle progression.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · CELL CYCLE CHECKPOINTS (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following statements about cellular senescence is correct?
options: Cellular senescence results in the death of cells due to excessive DNA damage.
Senescent cells can stimulate tumorigenesis by secreting substances that promote a harmful inflammatory response.
Fibroblasts undergo senescence after approximately 100 divisions in culture.
Telomerase is expressed at high levels in most normal somatic cells.
items.explanation: Senescent cells can stimulate tumorigenesis by secreting substances that promote a harmful inflammatory response.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · CELLULAR SENESCENCE

items.question: What is the role of telomerase in cancer cells?
options: Telomerase shortens telomeres, leading to cell death.
Telomerase prevents telomere shortening and allows indefinite cell proliferation.
Telomerase expression decreases with each cell division cycle.
Telomerase is not involved in the maintenance of telomeres.
items.explanation: Telomerase prevents telomere shortening and allows indefinite cell proliferation, which is crucial for cancer cells to bypass growth arrest.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · CELLULAR SENESCENCE

items.question: Which of the following best describes replicative senescence?
options: It occurs when cells divide indefinitely without any growth limitations.
It results from telomere shortening leading to a p53-regulated DNA-damage checkpoint response.
It is characterized by an increase in telomerase activity in normal somatic cells.
It involves the rapid division of fibroblasts beyond 50 cycles.
items.explanation: Replicative senescence results from telomere shortening leading to a p53-regulated DNA-damage checkpoint response, causing growth arrest.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · CELLULAR SENESCENCE

items.question: What is the consequence of critically short telomeres in normal cells?
options: They lead to indefinite cell proliferation.
They trigger a p53-regulated DNA-damage checkpoint response leading to growth arrest.
They cause an increase in telomerase activity.
They prevent any form of cellular senescence.
items.explanation: Critically short telomeres trigger a p53-regulated DNA-damage checkpoint response, leading to growth arrest and potentially cell death.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · CELLULAR SENESCENCE

items.subtopic: SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION PATHWAYS IN CANCER CELLS (part 1)
items.question: Which of the following kinases can phosphorylate both threonine and tyrosine residues?
items.explanation: MEK kinases can phosphorylate both threonine and tyrosine residues.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION PATHWAYS IN CANCER CELLS (part 1)

items.subtopic: SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION PATHWAYS IN CANCER CELLS (part 1)
items.question: Which pathway is NOT mentioned as important for malignant cells in the passage?
items.explanation: The Wnt signaling pathway is not mentioned in the passage.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION PATHWAYS IN CANCER CELLS (part 1)

items.subtopic: SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION PATHWAYS IN CANCER CELLS (part 1)
items.question: Which drug targets c-Abl and BCR gene translocations?
items.explanation: Imatinib is effective in tumors with c-Abl and BCR gene translocations.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION PATHWAYS IN CANCER CELLS (part 1)

items.subtopic: SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION PATHWAYS IN CANCER CELLS (part 1)
items.question: Which of the following inhibitors has activity against the T315I mutation?
items.explanation: Ponatinib is active against the T315I mutation.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION PATHWAYS IN CANCER CELLS (part 1)

items.subtopic: SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION PATHWAYS IN CANCER CELLS (part 2)
items.question: Which PI3K pathway inhibitor is specifically used for breast cancers with a mutation in the alpha isoform?
items.explanation: Alpelisib is the specific inhibitor of a mutation in the alpha isoform used for breast cancers with this mutation.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION PATHWAYS IN CANCER CELLS (part 2)

items.subtopic: SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION PATHWAYS IN CANCER CELLS (part 2)
items.question: Which class of inhibitors is active in renal cell carcinoma (RCC) and well-differentiated nonfunctional neuroendocrine tumors?
items.explanation: Inhibitors of mammalian target of rapamycin (mTOR) are active in RCC and well-differentiated nonfunctional neuroendocrine tumors.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION PATHWAYS IN CANCER CELLS (part 2)

items.subtopic: SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION PATHWAYS IN CANCER CELLS (part 2)
items.question: What is a significant advantage of testing blood for circulating tumor DNA over tumor biopsies?
options: It samples only one site of the tumor.
It can detect genetic abnormalities across all metastatic lesions.
items.explanation: Testing blood for circulating tumor DNA allows sampling of all tumor sites, not just a single site.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION PATHWAYS IN CANCER CELLS (part 2)

items.subtopic: SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION PATHWAYS IN CANCER CELLS (part 2)
items.question: Which mutation in the BCR-ABL kinase prevents imatinib binding?
items.explanation: The threonine to isoleucine change at codon 315 (T315I) in the BCR-ABL kinase prevents imatinib binding.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION PATHWAYS IN CANCER CELLS (part 2)

items.subtopic: SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION PATHWAYS IN CANCER CELLS (part 3)
items.question: Which combination has shown significant activity against HER2-positive gastric and esophageal cancers?
items.explanation: The passage mentions that combinations of chemotherapy, trastuzumab, and the immunotherapeutic agent pembrolizumab have shown significant activity against HER2-positive gastric and esophageal cancers.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION PATHWAYS IN CANCER CELLS (part 3)

items.subtopic: SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION PATHWAYS IN CANCER CELLS (part 3)
items.question: What is a major challenge in targeting mutations in the ras family of genes?
options: They are not common in oncogenes.
RAS proteins are kinases and difficult to inhibit.
Mechanisms related to RAS structure, activation, and inactivation.
They do not play a role in downstream signaling pathways.
items.explanation: The passage states that mutations in ras have proved to be very difficult targets due to mechanisms related to the structure of RAS proteins as well as their activation (GTP bound) and inactivation (GDP bound).
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION PATHWAYS IN CANCER CELLS (part 3)

items.subtopic: SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION PATHWAYS IN CANCER CELLS (part 3)
items.question: What is oncogene addiction?
options: A tumor cell's reliance on multiple pathways for survival.
A dominant pathway for survival and growth due to an activating mutation in an oncogene.
The activation of NF-κB by proteasome inhibitors.
items.explanation: Oncogene addiction refers to a situation where a tumor cell develops an activating mutation in an oncogene that becomes the dominant pathway for survival and growth, making it vulnerable to inhibitors of that pathway.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION PATHWAYS IN CANCER CELLS (part 3)

items.subtopic: SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION PATHWAYS IN CANCER CELLS (part 3)
items.question: How do proteasome inhibitors potentially produce anticancer effects?
options: By activating NF-κB in the nucleus.
By blocking the degradation of IκB, preventing NF-κB activation.
By enhancing the phosphorylation of IκB by IKK.
items.explanation: Proteasome inhibitors are thought to produce anticancer effects by blocking the proteolysis of IκB, thereby preventing NF-κB activation and its subsequent promotion of tumor cell survival.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION PATHWAYS IN CANCER CELLS (part 3)

items.subtopic: SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION PATHWAYS IN CANCER CELLS (part 4)
items.question: Which class of drugs has shown activity in patients with multiple myeloma by inhibiting proteasomes?
items.explanation: Proteasome inhibitors like bortezomib have activity in multiple myeloma.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION PATHWAYS IN CANCER CELLS (part 4)

items.subtopic: SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION PATHWAYS IN CANCER CELLS (part 4)
items.question: What is the primary mechanism of action for selective estrogen receptor modulators (SERMs) like tamoxifen in treating ER-positive breast cancer?
options: Modulating transcriptional activity of estrogen receptors
items.explanation: SERMs modulate the transcriptional activity of estrogen receptors.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION PATHWAYS IN CANCER CELLS (part 4)

items.subtopic: SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION PATHWAYS IN CANCER CELLS (part 4)
items.question: What is a common side effect associated with tamoxifen use in breast cancer treatment?
items.explanation: Tamoxifen can increase the risk of uterine cancer.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION PATHWAYS IN CANCER CELLS (part 4)

items.subtopic: CANCER-SPECIFIC GENETIC CHANGES AND SYNTHETIC LETHALITY
items.question: Which of the following best describes the concept of oncogene addiction in cancer cells?
options: Dependence on pathways containing activated oncogenes for proliferation.
Resistance to inhibition of specific oncogenic pathways.
Restoration of normal function of tumor-suppressor genes.
Enhanced cell survival mechanisms through overexpression of Bcl-2.
items.explanation: Oncogene addiction refers to cancer cells' dependence on signaling pathways containing activated oncogenes for proliferation.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · CANCER-SPECIFIC GENETIC CHANGES AND SYNTHETIC LETHALITY

items.subtopic: CANCER-SPECIFIC GENETIC CHANGES AND SYNTHETIC LETHALITY
items.question: What is the primary challenge in targeting tumor-suppressor gene defects?
options: The target of mutation is often deleted.
It is easy to restore normal function than to inhibit abnormal protein function.
Targeting specific oncogenic pathways leads to resistance.
Enhanced cell killing by targeting synthetic lethal genes.
items.explanation: Targeting tumor-suppressor gene defects is challenging because the target of mutation is often deleted, making it difficult to restore normal function.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · CANCER-SPECIFIC GENETIC CHANGES AND SYNTHETIC LETHALITY

items.subtopic: CANCER-SPECIFIC GENETIC CHANGES AND SYNTHETIC LETHALITY
items.question: Which therapeutic approach has been approved for treating cancers with BRCA1 mutations?
options: PARP inhibitors targeting single-strand break DNA repair.
Restoration of normal function in mutant suppressor genes.
items.explanation: PARP inhibitors, which target single-strand break DNA repair, have been approved for treating cancers with BRCA1 mutations.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · CANCER-SPECIFIC GENETIC CHANGES AND SYNTHETIC LETHALITY

items.subtopic: EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON CANCER GENE TRANSCRIPTION (part 1)
items.question: Which of the following is a common mechanism by which tumor-suppressor loci are epigenetically silenced in cancer cells?
items.explanation: Hypermethylation of DNA promoter regions is a common mechanism for epigenetic silencing of tumor-suppressor genes.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON CANCER GENE TRANSCRIPTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON CANCER GENE TRANSCRIPTION (part 1)
items.question: What role does histone acetylation play in gene transcription?
options: It induces a closed chromatin conformation that represses transcription.
It recruits HDACs to prevent transcription initiation.
It induces an open chromatin conformation that promotes transcription initiation.
items.explanation: Acetylation of histones H3 and H4 induces an open chromatin structure, promoting transcription initiation.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON CANCER GENE TRANSCRIPTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON CANCER GENE TRANSCRIPTION (part 1)
items.question: Which genes can be epigenetically silenced in sporadic forms of renal, breast, and colon cancer?
items.explanation: In sporadic renal, breast, and colon cancer, VHL, BRCA1, and STK11 genes can be epigenetically silenced.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON CANCER GENE TRANSCRIPTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON CANCER GENE TRANSCRIPTION (part 1)
items.question: Which of the following is a potential advantage of using DNA methylation analysis in cancer detection?
options: It identifies genetic mutations with high specificity.
It provides a more sensitive and specific approach than mutational analysis alone.
It detects all types of epigenetic changes.
It can only detect hypomethylation events.
items.explanation: DNA methylation analysis offers a potentially more sensitive and specific method for cancer detection compared to DNA mutational analysis alone.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON CANCER GENE TRANSCRIPTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON CANCER GENE TRANSCRIPTION (part 2)
items.question: Which fusion protein, resulting from a chromosomal translocation in acute promyelocytic leukemia (APL), recruits HDACs to inhibit gene expression?
items.explanation: The PML-RAR α fusion protein, from the t(15;17) translocation in APL, recruits HDACs to inhibit gene expression.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON CANCER GENE TRANSCRIPTION (part 2)

items.subtopic: EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON CANCER GENE TRANSCRIPTION (part 2)
items.question: What is a therapeutic effect of all-trans retinoic acid (ATRA) in the treatment of APL?
options: Releases HDAC activity and recruits coactivators
items.explanation: ATRA releases HDAC activity and recruits coactivators, overcoming the differentiation block in APL.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON CANCER GENE TRANSCRIPTION (part 2)

items.subtopic: EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON CANCER GENE TRANSCRIPTION (part 2)
items.question: Which of the following is a mechanism by which HDAC inhibitors exert antitumor activity?
options: Upregulation of proteins that inhibit apoptosis
items.explanation: HDAC inhibitors enhance apoptosis by upregulating death receptors among other mechanisms.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON CANCER GENE TRANSCRIPTION (part 2)

items.subtopic: EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON CANCER GENE TRANSCRIPTION (part 2)
items.question: Which drug is approved for use in myelodysplastic syndrome due to its ability to induce DNA demethylation?
items.explanation: 5-aza-2-deoxycytidine is approved for use in myelodysplastic syndrome due to its DNA demethylation properties.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON CANCER GENE TRANSCRIPTION (part 2)

items.subtopic: APOPTOSIS AND OTHER MECHANISMS OF CELL DEATH (part 1)
items.question: Which of the following proteins are involved in blocking the function of inhibitor of apoptosis proteins (IAPs)?
items.explanation: SMAC binds to and blocks the function of IAPs, which are negative regulators of caspase activation.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · APOPTOSIS AND OTHER MECHANISMS OF CELL DEATH (part 1)

items.subtopic: APOPTOSIS AND OTHER MECHANISMS OF CELL DEATH (part 1)
items.question: Which pathway is initiated by the release of cytochrome c and SMAC from the mitochondrial intermembrane space?
items.explanation: The intrinsic pathway of apoptosis is initiated by the release of cytochrome c and SMAC from the mitochondrial intermembrane space.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · APOPTOSIS AND OTHER MECHANISMS OF CELL DEATH (part 1)

items.subtopic: APOPTOSIS AND OTHER MECHANISMS OF CELL DEATH (part 1)
items.question: What triggers the extrinsic pathway of apoptosis?
options: Cross-linking of TNF receptor superfamily members by their ligands
items.explanation: The extrinsic pathway is activated by cross-linking members of the TNF receptor superfamily, such as CD95 (Fas) and death receptors DR4 and DR5, by their ligands.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · APOPTOSIS AND OTHER MECHANISMS OF CELL DEATH (part 1)

items.subtopic: APOPTOSIS AND OTHER MECHANISMS OF CELL DEATH (part 1)
items.question: Which of the following is NOT a regulated cell death process mentioned in the passage?
items.explanation: Necrosis is not listed as one of the regulated cell death processes; instead, necroptosis is mentioned.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · APOPTOSIS AND OTHER MECHANISMS OF CELL DEATH (part 1)

items.subtopic: APOPTOSIS AND OTHER MECHANISMS OF CELL DEATH (part 2)
items.question: Which of the following compounds is an oral BH3 mimetic inhibitor approved for use in patients with refractory CLL?
items.explanation: Venetoclax is an oral BH3 mimetic inhibitor of BCL-2 approved for use in patients with refractory CLL.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · APOPTOSIS AND OTHER MECHANISMS OF CELL DEATH (part 2)

items.subtopic: APOPTOSIS AND OTHER MECHANISMS OF CELL DEATH (part 2)
items.question: What mechanism allows normal cells to resist apoptosis induced by TRAIL, unlike tumor cells?
options: Expression of decoy receptors or elevated levels of intracellular inhibitors like FLIP
items.explanation: Normal cells resist TRAIL-induced apoptosis through expression of decoy receptors or elevated levels of intracellular inhibitors such as FLIP.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · APOPTOSIS AND OTHER MECHANISMS OF CELL DEATH (part 2)

items.subtopic: APOPTOSIS AND OTHER MECHANISMS OF CELL DEATH (part 2)
items.question: Which family member is a prototype ATP-dependent efflux pump associated with multidrug resistance?
items.explanation: P-glycoprotein (PGP) is the prototype member of the ABC family associated with multidrug resistance.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · APOPTOSIS AND OTHER MECHANISMS OF CELL DEATH (part 2)

items.subtopic: APOPTOSIS AND OTHER MECHANISMS OF CELL DEATH (part 2)
items.question: In cancer cells, what role does autophagy play when they are stressed by chemotherapy?
options: Serves as a mechanism of survival for cancer cells
Has no effect on cancer cell survival
items.explanation: In established tumors, autophagy may serve as a mechanism of survival for cancer cells when they are stressed by chemotherapy.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · APOPTOSIS AND OTHER MECHANISMS OF CELL DEATH (part 2)

items.question: Which process allows cancer cells to migrate by losing epithelial characteristics and gaining mesenchymal traits?
items.explanation: Epithelial Mesenchymal Transition (EMT) is the process by which cancer cells lose epithelial properties and gain mesenchymal traits, facilitating migration.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · METASTASIS (part 1)

items.question: What must metastatic cells suppress to avoid programmed cell death after losing contact with the ECM?
items.explanation: Metastatic cells must suppress anoikis, which is apoptosis induced by loss of contact with the extracellular matrix (ECM).
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · METASTASIS (part 1)

items.question: Which molecule can enhance the metastatic potential of breast cancer cells and is targeted by trastuzumab?
items.explanation: HER2 enhances the metastatic potential of breast cancer cells, and trastuzumab targets HER2 to improve survival.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · METASTASIS (part 1)

items.question: What is a critical step for metastasis that involves tumor cells adapting to a new microenvironment?
options: Survival and expansion in the novel microenvironment
items.explanation: The rate-limiting step for metastasis is the ability of tumor cells to survive and expand in the novel microenvironment of the metastatic site.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · METASTASIS (part 1)

items.question: Which cytokine increases RANK signaling and promotes osteoclast activity in bone metastases?
items.explanation: Interleukin (IL)1 is one of the substances secreted by tumors that increases RANK signaling and promotes osteoclast activity.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · METASTASIS (part 2) | CANCER STEM CELLS

items.question: What role does OPG play in bone metastases?
items.explanation: OPG acts as a decoy receptor to inhibit RANK activation, thus reducing osteoclast activity.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · METASTASIS (part 2) | CANCER STEM CELLS

items.question: Which treatment is used to prevent further bone destruction in cancer patients with bone metastases?
items.explanation: Inhibition of RANKL by an antibody (denosumab) can prevent further bone destruction.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · METASTASIS (part 2) | CANCER STEM CELLS

items.question: What characterizes the phenotype of cancer stem cells in AML and CML?
items.explanation: Cancer stem cells in AML and CML have an undifferentiated phenotype characterized as Thy1-CD34+CD38-.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · METASTASIS (part 2) | CANCER STEM CELLS

items.question: Which of the following mechanisms is NOT mentioned as a way cancer cells develop resistance to therapy?
options: Inhibiting drug delivery to the cancer cells
items.explanation: The passage does not mention enhancing immune system recognition as a mechanism of resistance.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · PLASTICITY AND RESISTANCE

items.question: What is a major problem in cancer therapy according to the passage?
options: Cancer cells lack plasticity and adaptability.
Tumors are homogeneous, making treatment straightforward.
Malignancies have multiple mechanisms for resistance.
All cancers can be cured with chemotherapy.
items.explanation: The passage states that a major problem is the wide spectrum of mechanisms for both initial and adaptive resistance to treatments.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · PLASTICITY AND RESISTANCE

items.question: Which factor contributes to cancer cell plasticity as mentioned in the passage?
options: Heterogeneity between different clones of cells within a tumor.
Lack of interaction with the tumor microenvironment.
Absence of mutations in target proteins.
items.explanation: The passage mentions heterogeneity between different clones and their interactions as contributing to cancer cell plasticity.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · PLASTICITY AND RESISTANCE

items.question: Which metabolic pathway is primarily utilized by many cancer cells to metabolize glucose, leading to increased lactic acid production?
items.explanation: Many cancer cells utilize aerobic glycolysis, also known as the Warburg effect, to metabolize glucose.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · CANCER METABOLISM

items.question: Which protein complex is inhibited by AMP-activated kinase (AMPK) in controlling the glycolytic process?
items.explanation: AMPK inhibits mTORC1, a protein complex involved in controlling the glycolytic process.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · CANCER METABOLISM

items.question: Mutations in which genes are frequently found in gliomas, AMLs, and intrahepatic cholangiocarcinomas, leading to the production of an oncometabolite?
items.explanation: Mutations in isocitrate dehydrogenases 1 and 2 (IDH1 and IDH2) are frequently found in these cancers.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · CANCER METABOLISM

items.question: Which oncometabolite is produced due to mutations in IDH1 and IDH2, acting as a competitive inhibitor for α-ketoglutarate?
items.explanation: Mutations in IDH1 and IDH2 lead to the production of 2-hydroxyglutarate (2HG), which inhibits α-ketoglutarate.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · CANCER METABOLISM

items.subtopic: TUMOR MICROENVIRONMENT, ANGIOGENESIS, AND IMMUNE EVASION | OBESITY AND CANCER
items.question: Which of the following is NOT a mechanism by which the tumor microenvironment contributes to drug resistance?
options: Induction of an inflammatory environment that promotes apoptosis.
Alteration in metabolic pathways creating a hypoxic and acidic environment.
items.explanation: The tumor microenvironment induces an inflammatory environment, but this does not promote apoptosis; rather, it inhibits effector T-cell access to malignant cells.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · TUMOR MICROENVIRONMENT, ANGIOGENESIS, AND IMMUNE EVASION | OBESITY AND CANCER

items.subtopic: TUMOR MICROENVIRONMENT, ANGIOGENESIS, AND IMMUNE EVASION | OBESITY AND CANCER
items.question: Which of the following factors is associated with obesity and may contribute to cancer development?
options: Increased levels of IL-6 and TNF-alpha.
items.explanation: Obesity is associated with increased levels of inflammatory proteins such as IL-6 and TNF-alpha, contributing to an inflammatory environment that may promote cancer development.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · TUMOR MICROENVIRONMENT, ANGIOGENESIS, AND IMMUNE EVASION | OBESITY AND CANCER

items.subtopic: TUMOR MICROENVIRONMENT, ANGIOGENESIS, AND IMMUNE EVASION | OBESITY AND CANCER
items.question: In the context of obesity and cancer, which statement is true regarding immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy?
options: It is less effective in obese patients.
Its effectiveness is independent of patient weight.
It has been observed to be more effective in obese patients.
Obesity negates the effects of this therapy.
items.explanation: Immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy appears to be more effective in obese patients, suggesting a complex relationship between obesity and immune response.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · TUMOR MICROENVIRONMENT, ANGIOGENESIS, AND IMMUNE EVASION | OBESITY AND CANCER

items.question: Which factor is primarily responsible for stimulating the angiogenic switch in tumors?
options: Genetic lesions in oncogenes or tumor suppressors
items.explanation: The angiogenic switch is stimulated by hypoxemia, inflammation, and genetic lesions.

items.explanation: The assertion is true as defects in telomerase cause dyskeratosis congenita, but the reason is false because dyskeratosis congenita involves abnormalities in rapidly dividing cells and not nutrient absorption.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · CELLULAR SENESCENCE

items.subtopic: SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION PATHWAYS IN CANCER CELLS (part 1)
items.assertion: Inhibition of kinase activity can be effective in treating certain cancers.
items.reason: Many human cancers have mutations that lead to enhanced and/or prolonged kinase pathway activity.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains why inhibition is effective.

items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION PATHWAYS IN CANCER CELLS (part 1)

items.subtopic: SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION PATHWAYS IN CANCER CELLS (part 2)
items.assertion: Resistance to kinase inhibitors often develops in patients.

items.assertion: Testing blood for circulating tumor DNA is limited to detecting mutations at a single site.

items.assertion: Proteasome inhibitors activate NF-κB by promoting its degradation.

items.assertion: Identifying synthetic lethal relationships can enhance cell killing in oncogene-addicted pathways.

items.assertion: Epigenetic events are potentially reversible and amenable to therapeutic intervention.
items.reason: Unlike genetic mutations, epigenetic changes do not alter the DNA sequence.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; the reversibility of epigenetic changes is due to their non-sequence altering nature.

items.assertion: Histone acetylation leads to transcriptional repression.

items.subtopic: APOPTOSIS AND OTHER MECHANISMS OF CELL DEATH (part 1)
items.assertion: Cancer cells often develop mechanisms to inhibit apoptosis.
items.reason: This inhibition allows cancer cells to evade programmed cell death and enhances their survival.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why cancer cells inhibit apoptosis.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · APOPTOSIS AND OTHER MECHANISMS OF CELL DEATH (part 1)

items.subtopic: APOPTOSIS AND OTHER MECHANISMS OF CELL DEATH (part 1)
items.assertion: The extrinsic pathway of apoptosis is initiated by DNA damage.
items.reason: DNA damage leads to the release of cytochrome c from mitochondria.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the extrinsic pathway is activated by ligand-receptor interactions, not DNA damage. The reason describes the intrinsic pathway.
items.reference: Cancer Cell Biology · APOPTOSIS AND OTHER MECHANISMS OF CELL DEATH (part 1)

items.subtopic: APOPTOSIS AND OTHER MECHANISMS OF CELL DEATH (part 2)
items.assertion: Autophagy plays a dual role in cancer development and survival.
items.reason: Early in carcinogenesis, autophagy acts as a tumor suppressor; in established tumors, it helps cells survive stress from chemotherapy.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
